questions.question: On a lateral chest radiograph, how can the radiologist distinguish the left hemidiaphragm from the right?
choices: The left hemidiaphragm is always the highest point.
The left hemidiaphragm is seen much further anteriorly.
The right hemidiaphragm typically has a stomach bubble beneath it.
The heart touches the left hemidiaphragm, making its anterior border disappear.
The right hemidiaphragm is usually lower than the left.
questions.detail: The heart touches the left hemidiaphragm, making its anterior border disappear. This is the silhouette sign.

questions.question: Why do the right ribs appear larger and more posterior than the left ribs on a standard lateral chest X-ray?
choices: The right ribs are naturally larger in the human skeleton.
Lateral CXRs are taken in the left lateral position, causing geometric magnification of the right-sided structures.
The left ribs are displaced anteriorly by the heart.
The right ribs are closer to the X-ray film/cassette.
The left ribs are smaller due to the presence of the left lung.
questions.detail: Lateral CXRs are taken in the left lateral position, causing geometric magnification. The farther something is from the film, the more magnified it is.

questions.question: In the lateral view of the hilum, what structure is referred to as the 'Dark Hole'?
questions.detail: The left upper lobe bronchus represents 'looking down the barrel'.

questions.question: When identifying the pulmonary arteries on a lateral CXR, which of the following is true?
choices: The left pulmonary artery passes anterior to the right main bronchus.
The right pulmonary artery is located superior to the left pulmonary artery.
The left pulmonary artery hooks up and over the left main bronchus.
The right pulmonary artery is the 'loser in the back.'
The left pulmonary artery is always lower than the right.
questions.detail: The left pulmonary artery hooks up and over the left main bronchus. 'L'osers in the back refers to the Left being posterior.

questions.question: An opacity in the Retrotracheal Triangle (Raider Triangle) on a lateral CXR is a classic indicator of:
questions.detail: An aberrant right subclavian artery is the classic cause of an obliterated retrotracheal triangle.

questions.question: The 'Raider Triangle' is bordered anteriorly by which structure?
choices: The posterior wall of the trachea.
questions.detail: It is bordered anteriorly by the posterior wall of the trachea and posteriorly by the spine.

questions.question: On a frontal CXR, the horizontal ribs are those that are:
questions.detail: The posterior ribs appear more horizontal. 'Horizon is in the back'.

questions.question: For a hilum to be considered normal on a frontal CXR, the left hilar point should be approximately how much higher than the right hilar point?
questions.detail: The left hilar point should always be around 1 cm higher than the right.

questions.question: How many layers of pleura are involved in an Azygos Lobe Fissure?
questions.detail: The azygos lobe fissure contains 4 layers of pleura.

questions.question: On the right side, how many bronchopulmonary segments are typically present?
questions.detail: There are 10 bronchopulmonary segments on the right.

questions.question: Which lobe on the left side is composed of four segments?
questions.detail: On the left, both the upper lobe/lingula and the lower lobe typically have 4 segments each.

questions.question: A 'Pig Bronchus' (Tracheal Bronchus) is a variant airway that most commonly results in recurrent pneumonia in which lobe?
questions.detail: 'Pig Pneumonia' refers to recurrent RUL pneumonia in a child due to this variant.

questions.question: Which variant airway is described as a 'blind ending' structure and is considered the only true supernumerary bronchus?
questions.detail: The cardiac bronchus is often blind-ending and is the only true supernumerary bronchus.

questions.question: According to the text, what is the most anterior structure in the superior mediastinum?
questions.detail: The thymus is the most anterior structure in the superior mediastinum.

questions.question: Which mediastinal compartment contains the roots of the central vessels and the bifurcation of the trachea?
questions.detail: The middle mediastinum contains the roots of central vessels and the tracheal bifurcation.

questions.question: The posterior mediastinum is defined as the space from the back of the heart to the spine and contains which structure?
questions.detail: The descending aorta is located in the posterior mediastinum along with the esophagus and thoracic duct.

questions.question: What is the most common supernumerary pulmonary vein?
questions.detail: The right middle is the most common supernumerary pulmonary vein.

questions.question: A 'Common Ostia' of the pulmonary veins most typically involves the merging of:
choices: The right superior and inferior veins.
The right middle and inferior veins.
The left superior and inferior veins.
The left superior and right superior veins.
questions.detail: A common ostia typically involves the left superior and inferior veins merging before entering the atrium.

questions.question: Which pulmonary vein sleeve is the most common site for arrhythmogenic foci causing atrial fibrillation?
questions.detail: The left superior pulmonary vein is the most common site for a-fib foci.

questions.question: Accidental ligation of a 'Common Ostia' during surgery is a risk during which procedure?
questions.detail: Identification of a common ostia on pre-op CT is needed to avoid accidental ligation during a lobectomy.

questions.question: In the secondary pulmonary lobule, which structures run through the center?
questions.detail: The airway (terminal bronchus) and artery run down the center of the secondary pulmonary lobule.

questions.question: Where are the lymphatics located within the secondary pulmonary lobule?
choices: In the center with the artery.
In the periphery, outlining the septa.
questions.detail: Lymphatics and veins are found in the periphery, outlining the septa.

questions.question: The terminal bronchial and its accompanying artery in the lobule center are surrounded by:
questions.detail: The secondary pulmonary lobule (SPL) is the smallest part of the lung surrounded by connective tissue.

questions.question: The inferior border of the superior mediastinum is drawn from the sternal-manubrial junction to which vertebral level?
questions.detail: The line is drawn from the sternal-manubrial junction to the T4 vertebral level.

questions.question: Which structure is located in the middle mediastinum?
choices: The Bulk of the Thymus (in kids).
questions.detail: The phrenic nerves are part of the middle mediastinum.

questions.question: Which pattern of atelectasis results from a loss of surface tension due to a surfactant deficiency?
questions.detail: Adhesive atelectasis results from a loss of surface tension or surfactant deficiency.

questions.question: 'Resorption atelectasis' is another name for which subtype?
questions.detail: Obstructive atelectasis is also known as absorptive or resorption atelectasis.

questions.question: A patient with a large pleural effusion is most likely to demonstrate which type of atelectasis in the adjacent lung?
questions.detail: Compressive atelectasis is classically seen adjacent to a pleural effusion.

questions.question: Which type of atelectasis is most classically associated with Tuberculosis (TB)?
questions.detail: Fibrotic atelectasis is also known as cicatrization and is classic for TB.

questions.question: In the setting of a pulmonary embolism, which mechanism leads to adhesive atelectasis?
choices: Loss of blood flow and CO2 disrupting surfactant integrity.
questions.detail: In PE, the loss of blood flow and CO2 disrupts surfactant integrity, leading to adhesive atelectasis.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered a DIRECT sign of atelectasis?
questions.detail: Displacement of the fissures is considered one of the most dependable direct signs.

questions.question: What is the earliest direct sign of volume loss in an atelectatic area?
questions.detail: Crowding of vessels and bronchi is considered one of the earliest direct signs.

questions.question: The 'S Sign of Golden' is a classic radiographic finding associated with:
choices: Left upper lobe collapse due to a hilar mass.
Right upper lobe collapse due to a central obstructing mass.
Right lower lobe collapse in a smoker.
Total lung collapse from a mucus plug.
questions.detail: The 'S Sign of Golden' refers to the reverse 'S' shape seen in RUL collapse caused by a central mass.

questions.question: In Right Upper Lobe (RUL) atelectasis, how does the lobe typically 'close'?
choices: It collapses posteriorly against the spine.
It collapses inferiorly towards the diaphragm.
It closes like a fan attached at the hilum.
It moves medially to obscure the heart border.
It displaces the major fissure posteriorly.
questions.detail: The specific lobar pattern for RUL collapse is that it closes like a fan attached at the hilum.

questions.question: A patient presents with increased density at the right heart border and loss of the heart border silhouette. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Loss of the right heart border is the classic look for RML collapse.

questions.question: 'Lady Windermere Syndrome' is a clinical scenario involving chronic collapse of the RML associated with which infection?
questions.detail: Lady Windermere syndrome involves MAI; the nickname suggests she is 'too proper to cough.'

questions.question: What is the critical distinction between RML collapse and RLL collapse on a frontal CXR?
choices: RML collapse obscures the right hemidiaphragm.
RLL collapse obscures the right heart border.
RLL collapse maintains the right heart border silhouette.
RML collapse creates a 'Superior Triangle Sign.'
RLL collapse bows the minor fissure upward.
questions.detail: In RLL collapse, the heart border silhouette should NOT be lost, whereas it is lost in RML collapse.

questions.question: The 'Superior Triangle Sign' represents the movement of mediastinal vessels in the setting of:
questions.detail: RLL collapse creates the 'Superior Triangle Sign' to the right of the trachea.

questions.question: If both the right hemidiaphragm and the right heart border silhouettes are lost, what is the most likely combination of collapsed lobes?
questions.detail: Loss of both visualization points (heart border and diaphragm) suggests a combined RML and RLL collapse.

questions.question: The 'Luftsichel Sign' is a lucent stripe seen in which condition?
questions.detail: 'Luftsichel' means 'Air Sickle' and is seen in Left Upper Lobe collapse.

questions.question: The 'Luftsichel' appearance is caused by the hyperinflation of which lung segment?
choices: Superior (apical) segment of the Left Lower Lobe.
questions.detail: The superior (apical) segment of the Left Lower Lobe becomes hyperinflated and pinned between the aorta and the collapsed LUL segment.

questions.question: Which radiographic sign is described as a flattened appearance of the left heart border and hilum in the setting of LLL collapse?
questions.detail: The 'Flat Waist Sign' describes the flattened contours of the hilum and heart border in LLL collapse.

questions.question: On a frontal CXR, Left Lower Lobe collapse can be 'sneaky' because:
choices: It always resembles a pleural effusion.
The opacity is often hidden behind the heart.
It displaces the trachea to the right.
It bows the minor fissure superiorly.
questions.detail: LLL collapse is 'sneaky' on frontal views because the opacity is often hidden behind the heart.

questions.question: Acute atelectasis typically favors which type of displacement?
choices: Compensatory overinflation of the normal lung.
Shift of the minor fissure only.
Posterior shift of the major fissure.
questions.detail: Acute atelectasis favors diaphragmatic and mediastinal displacement.

questions.question: Chronic atelectasis is more likely to show which secondary finding?
questions.detail: Chronic atelectasis is more likely to show compensatory overinflation of the non-atelectatic lung.

questions.question: The loss of an interface seen when two objects of similar density touch is known as:
questions.detail: The Silhouette Sign describes the loss of an interface.

questions.question: The 'Hilum Overlay Sign' is used to determine if a mass is:
choices: In the hilum or anterior/posterior to it.
questions.detail: The Hilum Overlay Sign is an application of the silhouette sign used to see if a mass is in the hilum or elsewhere.

questions.question: If the hilar vessels are obscured by a soft tissue mass, the mass is located:
questions.detail: If vessels are obscured, the soft tissue mass is in the hilum, touching them.

questions.question: If hilar vessels can still be clearly seen through a mass, the mass is located:
choices: Anterior or posterior to the hilum.
questions.detail: If vessels can be seen through the mass, the mass is anterior or posterior to the hilum and not touching them.

questions.question: The 'Cervicothoracic Sign' relies on the anatomical fact that:
choices: The posterior mediastinum ends at the clavicles.
The anterior mediastinum ends at the clavicles.
The trachea bifurcates at the clavicles.
The esophagus is anterior to the trachea at the clavicles.
The aortic arch is above the clavicles.
questions.detail: The basis for the cervicothoracic sign is that the anterior mediastinum ends at the clavicles.

questions.question: A mass that extends above the level of the clavicles on a frontal CXR is NOT located in the:
questions.detail: Anything above the clavicles is NOT in the anterior mediastinum.

questions.question: What is a classic example of a mass that frequently demonstrates a positive Cervicothoracic Sign?
questions.detail: A thyroid goiter is a classic example.

questions.question: Which of the following structures extends above the clavicles, unlike its anterior counterpart?
questions.detail: The posterior junction line extends above the clavicles.

questions.question: The 'Incomplete Border Sign' is used to differentiate:
choices: Pulmonary masses from pleural/chest wall masses.
questions.detail: It is used to differentiate pulmonary masses (with crisp borders) from pleural/chest wall masses.

questions.question: A pulmonary mass (completely surrounded by lung) should typically have which type of border?
choices: Tapered at an acute angle with the pleura.
questions.detail: A pulmonary mass typically has a crisp and circumferential border.

questions.question: Which bacterial pathogen is the most common cause of pneumonia in AIDS patients?
questions.detail: Strep Pneumonia is the most common cause of pneumonia in AIDS patients.

questions.question: 'Lobar consolidation favoring the lower lobes' is the classic description for which bacterial pneumonia?
questions.detail: Lobar consolidation favoring the lower lobes is classic for Strep Pneumonia.

questions.question: Which infection is associated with endocarditis and can spread hematogenously to cause bilateral patchy opacities and abscesses?
questions.detail: Staph Aureus often presents as bilateral bronchopneumonia spread via blood in endocarditis.

questions.question: A CXR showing mediastinal widening and pleural effusions in a clinical setting of potential bio-terrorism is suspicious for:
questions.detail: Mediastinal widening in a bio-terrorism setting is the classic look for Anthrax.

questions.question: The 'Bulging Fissure' sign due to exuberant inflammation is the hallmark of which pneumonia?
questions.detail: The 'Bulging Fissure' is the hallmark buzzword for Klebsiella pneumonia.

questions.question: Which patient population is at the highest risk for Klebsiella pneumonia?
questions.detail: Alcoholics and nursing home patients are the classic demographic for Klebsiella.

questions.question: H. Flu pneumonia is most commonly seen in which underlying condition?
questions.detail: H. Flu pneumonia is commonly seen in patients with COPD and those without a spleen.

questions.question: Which bacterial infection is typical for ICU patients on a ventilator and often forms abscesses?
questions.detail: Pseudomonas is seen in ICU patients on ventilators.

questions.question: Legionella pneumonia is classically associated with which source?
questions.detail: Legionella is classically associated with contaminated air conditioners and is also seen in COPDers.

questions.question: Cavitation in Legionella pneumonia is typically seen ONLY in which patients?
questions.detail: Legionella only typically cavitates in immunosuppressed patients.

questions.question: In a supine patient, aspiration pneumonia most commonly involves which lung segments?
choices: Posterior segments of the upper lobes and superior segments of the lower lobes.
Anterior segments of the upper lobes.
questions.detail: In the supine position, aspiration favors the posterior segments of the upper lobes and superior segments of the lower lobes.

questions.question: What is the most common complication of aspiration pneumonia?
questions.detail: Empyema is the most common complication of aspiration.

questions.question: A clinical history of a 'dental procedure gone bad' followed by aggressive airspace disease in the peripheral lower lobes and rib osteomyelitis suggests:
questions.detail: Actinomycosis is the classic answer for a 'dental procedure gone bad.'

questions.question: Which is the most common community-acquired pneumonia in the 5 to 20-year-old age group?
questions.detail: Mycoplasma is the most common community-acquired pneumonia in the 5-20 year age group.

questions.question: Mycoplasma pneumonia is associated with which syndrome characterized by a 'unilateral lucent lung'?
questions.detail: Swyer-James syndrome is associated with Mycoplasma and a unilateral lucent lung.

questions.question: According to the text's 'Gamesmanship,' how long should you wait for a follow-up CXR to confirm clearance of pneumonia in an elderly patient?
questions.detail: The 'gamesmanship' recommendation is to wait 3 months for an elderly patient's follow-up.

questions.question: What is the most classic opportunistic infection in AIDS patients, often presenting as bilateral perihilar ground-glass opacities?
questions.detail: PCP is the most classic AIDS infection.

questions.question: Thin-walled cysts and a high risk of pneumothorax are characteristic findings of which AIDS-related infection?
questions.detail: PCP is characterized by ground glass, thin-walled cysts, and PTX.

questions.question: A 'Flame-Shaped' perihilar opacity in an AIDS patient is a buzzword for:
questions.detail: A 'Flame-Shaped' perihilar opacity is a buzzword for Kaposi Sarcoma.

questions.question: In an AIDS patient, persistent opacities on imaging should make you think of:
questions.detail: Lymphoma should be considered if there are persistent opacities in an AIDS patient.

questions.question: Which condition in children with HIV is associated with perivascular cysts and ground-glass opacities?
questions.detail: LIP is a cystic disease seen in kids with HIV.

questions.question: A calcified TB granuloma is known as a:
questions.detail: A Ghon Focus is a calcified TB granuloma.

questions.question: A 'Ranke Complex' in TB consists of a calcified granuloma plus:
questions.detail: A Ranke Complex consists of a calcified granuloma plus a calcified hilar node.

questions.question: Bulky hilar and paratracheal adenopathy in the setting of primary TB is most commonly seen in:
questions.detail: Nodal expansion in primary TB is most common in children.

questions.question: What is the classic location for reactivation (post-primary) TB?
choices: Apical and posterior upper lobes and superior lower lobes.
Anterior segments of the upper lobes.
questions.detail: Reactivation TB is classically located in the apical and posterior upper lobes and superior lower lobes.

questions.question: Cavitation is a hallmark feature of which stage of Tuberculosis?
questions.detail: A cavity is a classic look for post-primary (reactivation) TB.

questions.question: A 'Rasmussen Aneurysm' is a pseudoaneurysm of which structure in the setting of a TB cavity?
questions.detail: A Rasmussen pseudoaneurysm occurs in the pulmonary artery near a TB cavity.

questions.question: In an HIV patient, a 'reactive TB pattern' with cavitation is typically seen when the CD4 count is:
questions.detail: Cavitation (reactive TB pattern) occurs when the CD4 count is > 200.

questions.question: Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS) occurs in AIDS/TB patients after starting HAART and is treated with:
questions.detail: Steroids are the therapy for IRIS.

questions.question: 'Lady Windermere Disease' refers to a non-tuberculous mycobacterial (NTM) infection favoring middle lobe/lingular bronchiectasis in which demographic?
choices: Old white male smokers with COPD.
questions.detail: This disease refers to MAC in elderly women who 'do not cough.'

questions.question: 'Hot-tub lung' is a form of Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis caused by aerosolized NTM and presents with:
questions.detail: Hot-tub lung presents with ill-defined, ground-glass centrilobular nodules.

questions.question: Which NTM presentation favors old white male smokers and mimics reactivation TB?
questions.detail: The cavitary type of NTM favors old white men with COPD.

questions.question: 'Invasive Aspergillus' is typically seen in which patient population?
questions.detail: AIDS or transplant patients are risk groups for Invasive Aspergillus.

questions.question: The 'Halo Sign' (nodule with a ground-glass halo) in an immunocompromised patient is classic for:
questions.detail: The 'Halo Sign' (consolidative nodule with ground glass) is classic for Invasive Aspergillus.

questions.question: On CT, the 'Air Crescent Sign' appearing within a consolidative mass in an AIDS patient after 2-3 weeks of treatment signifies:
questions.detail: The 'Air Crescent Sign' appearing after treatment signifies healing/recovery as necrotic lung separates from parenchyma.

questions.question: 'Finger-in-glove' opacities in an asthmatic patient are the hallmark of:
questions.detail: 'Finger-in-glove' opacities in an asthmatic are the hallmark of ABPA.

questions.question: Which aggressive fungal infection should be suspected if there is invasion of the mediastinum, pleura, and chest wall in a 'bad diabetic'?
questions.detail: Mucormycosis is an aggressive infection seen in 'bad diabetics.'

questions.question: Severe COVID-19 disease is described on imaging as having what percentage of lung involvement within 24-48 hours?
questions.detail: Severe disease is defined as > 50% lung involvement.

questions.question: The 'Acute Stage' CT findings of COVID-19 are most classically:
questions.detail: Bilateral, peripheral ground-glass opacities are the classic acute findings for COVID-19.

questions.question: In the late 'Absorption Stage' (>14 days) of COVID-19, which appearance is common?
questions.detail: Late absorption phase findings include 'fibrous stripes' in previous disease regions.

questions.question: Septic emboli are most commonly found in which lung distribution?
choices: Lower lobe predominant (due to blood flow).
questions.detail: Septic emboli are lower lobe predominant due to blood flow.

questions.question: 'Lemierre Syndrome' refers to jugular vein thrombosis with septic emboli, typically caused by which bacteria?
questions.detail: Fusobacterium necrophorum causes the majority of Lemierre cases.

questions.question: The 'Feeding Vessel Sign' (a vessel heading into a nodule) is classic for septic emboli or:
questions.detail: Both septic emboli and hematogenous metastases show the feeding vessel sign.

questions.question: Which infection causes a 'Fine reticular pattern' on CXR and 'Tree-in-bud' on CT in 5-20 year-olds?
questions.detail: Mycoplasma presents with patchy opacity and tree-in-bud in youth.

questions.question: In the 'CAVITY' mnemonic for lung cavities, what does the 'V' stand for?
questions.detail: The 'V' stands for Vascular (Septic/Bland Emboli).
